text: Thank you for joining for another robotic hernia surgery video. Today we're going to be talking about ETEP approaches to complex flank hernias. This is a sixty-year-old man with a history of two flank incisions, one an open colostectomy and the other an extended incision for an open appendectomy. He presented with a multifocal hernia on the right abdomen

Let's get into the CT scan. You'll notice quite a significant neurovascular injury to the abdominal wall musculature, and then large multifocal defects involving small bowel and colon. The hernias were complex, and the two largest ones were about five by ten and eight by ten, but there was other smaller ones as well. Denervation injury. And you can see that the medial element of that large hernia extends pretty close to the linea alba, about three, four centimeters from linea alba. So I knew that I was going to need broad mesh coverage to extend medially.

The surgeon performs a left retromuscular lateral dissection to achieve crossover, aiming for at least ten centimeters of retromuscular coverage. The procedure involves ETEP placement and crossover at the midline. The small umbilical hernia is reduced and the defect is closed.
The surgeon encounters subcostal Swiss cheese defects during the crossover, identifying interparietal hernias with intact anterior sheath and muscle, providing access to the retromuscular plane.
The surgeon identifies omentum within the surgical field, indicating an intraperitoneal view. The team begins lysis of adhesions before proceeding with a limited contralateral dissection.

The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions, with a flap obstructing the view. The retromuscular dissection reveals neurovascular injuries to the rectus muscle, which appears atrophic and fat-replaced, contrasting with the contralateral side. Omental adhesions are reduced from the prior subcostal incision, with visible Swiss cheese defects in the posterior but no full thickness hernias.

The surgeon performs a unification of the retromuscular dissection, extending cephalad into an untouched plane and inferiorly into the subdiaphragmatic area. This dissection is crucial for addressing subcostal hernias and ensuring adequate space for broad mesh coverage.

These cases, because you are going up and down the entire abdominal wall, really require good bedside assistance, and we were lucky to have an awesome team this day. And then at this point, move forward intra-abdominal lysis of adhesions. Again, a lot of people look at ETAP and think extraperitoneal, but it's an access approach. It can help facilitate, for instance, in this case, reducing the need to double doc, but it doesn't mean that you can't do intra-abdominal work. And so here we are.

The surgeon manually reduces bowel from a large medial hernia and identifies a single adhesive band on the posterior aspect of the hernia sac. The patient is positioned with the left side down and in Trendelenburg position for optimal exposure. The surgeon then proceeds with retromuscular dissection, starting from a lower position, to safely perform lysis of adhesions and expose the hernias and abdominal wall.

The surgeon performs a bottoms-up TAR (Transversus Abdominis Release) by opening the fascia and dissecting into the retroinguinal space, visualizing the muscle. The upper portion has already been developed, creating a volcano sign due to the nature of the dissection and tissue mobilization. The hernia sac is transected at its neck without recruitment, and the rectus muscle dissection continues, addressing multiple small holes due to the multifocal nature of the hernia. The posterior closure is meticulously executed to prevent fascial dehiscence.

The surgeon completes the unification of the upper and lower dissection, focusing on developing a solid lateral dissection for circumferential mesh coverage around the defect. A loose flap is visible as the anterior fascial closure is performed using non-absorbable V-lock sutures. The insufflation pressure is set at ten, facilitating the re-approximation of flank hernias. Four V-lock sutures are used to address the lower hernias and subcostal defects, with visible poor musculature laterally.
The surgeon placates the subcostal incision, reconstructing the abdominal wall anatomy. They proceed with posterior closure using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, demonstrating meticulous sewing technique.

Again, I did not recruit a hernia sag and it came together without any tension. For mesh, we opted for a heavyweight Mesh, I'm sorry, but it's actually microporous. It is not a macroporous mesh here. A typo on that slide. But really making sure to get good lateral coverage. Placed our drain. We also had a subcutaneous drain. So this case went great. I think each type of approach can be applied for flank hernias. The complex anatomy related to these hernias and the neurovascular trauma from the past can make dissection confusing. And it's always important to remember that though ETEP is the way that we enter, it doesn't mean that we can't get into the peritoneal cavity. So focusing on making sure that you get an appropriate license of adhesions is critical. Thank you very much. I hope that this was helpful.
